observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the IC to conduct proper physical count of PPE and ensure that (1) demolished properties and PPE items which are impossible to be counted are not included in the RPCPPE; and (2) additions such as improvements, rehabilitations, or major repairs are reported in the appropriate PPE accounts;
Direct the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) to maintain PPELCs with complete references to facilitate appropriate reconciliation with the RPCPPE. This could also assist the AT to conduct alternative audit procedures; and
Require the GSO to prepare and maintain complete and proper PCs for PPE items in compliance with the MNGAS. We also recommend that GSO review and update PPE items issued to LGU personnel who have already separated with the MGG to establish proper accountability of such items.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the MAO to:
Ensure that the effects of MGG’s financial transactions are faithfully and accurately represented in the FS in compliance with IPSAS 1; and
Adhere strictly to the criteria provided by COA Circular No. 2015-009 and use the appropriate account titles when recording the Municipality’s financial transactions.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the MAO to consistently observe the application of accrual basis of accounting to recognize and record expenses in the period they are incurred, regardless of when payment is made.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the:
MA and the Municipal Treasurer (MT) to continue the regular and periodic reconciliation of the CIB balances per books and cashbook records against the monthly BS to attain a correct reporting of the said account’s balance in the FS; and
MA to ensure that monthly BRS for all bank accounts are prepared. If BS cannot be obtained, avail of/request alternative verified bank records as basis for the BRS preparation.

We recommended and the MDC, in coordination with the Local Finance Committee, agreed to prepare regularly the LDIP to ensure that all development PPAs funded from the 20% DF are aligned with the priorities of the municipality.
We also recommended and Management agreed to adhere strictly to the requirements of RA No. 7160 regarding changes in the annual budget and refrain from utilizing prior years’ unexpended balances of the 20% DF for other development PPAs without prior authority from the SB on the reversion and re-appropriation of the same.
We likewise reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation and Management agreed to rigorously maximize the utilization of the 20% DF and ensure the timely implementation of PPAs to attain the desired social, economic, and environmental outcomes crucial for improving quality of life.

We recommended that Management ensure compliance with the rules and regulation relative to the conduct of public officials and employees and avoid any transaction that may prejudice propriety.
We also recommended that Management provide justification why the purchased residential building which was demolished should not be disallowed in audit.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation and Management agreed to ensure that the identified priority PPAs are implemented as planned and the LDRRMF are utilized optimally within the target timeframes.
We also recommended and Management agreed to enjoin the LDRRM Officer to monitor closely the implementation of all PPAs under the LDRRMF to facilitate corrective action, if any, and the timely reprogramming and reallocation of the fund for its optimum utilization.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the ME to:
Ensure that the infrastructure projects are completed according to specifications and of robust quality that can withstand heavy rain conditions to avoid the need for additional repair works;
Adequately plan the schedule of construction works in order to minimize the number of days falling within the rainy season; and
Enforce a stricter and more urgent demand whenever repair works are needed. Further, emphasize to the contractors to only use construction materials that are of adequate grade and quality.

We recommended and the concerned Departments and the Bids and Awards Committee agreed to:
Ensure that pre-procurement activities are undertaken at earlier timeframes. Moreover, to make certain that issues and concerns have been settled before actual project implementation; and
Plan more thoroughly the timing of procurement activities and actual project implementation while considering weather conditions to ensure that projects are completed within the targeted schedule.

We recommended and Management agreed to strengthen its gender mainstreaming efforts by ensuring that all PPAs included in the GPB are implemented.
We also recommended and the GAD Focal Person agreed to ensure that the GAD AR is prepared based on the approved GPB.

We recommended and LCE agreed to require the MPMIU to:
Plan adequately to achieve the earlier release of funds to the LGU and subsequent payments to the contractor while also ensuring accurate reporting of the project’s actual physical completion compared with its recorded cost;
Devise additional policies or procedures to eliminate unnecessary delays that are within the LGU’s control in the processing of documents for the release of funds from the PRDP and subsequent payments of the same to the contractor; and
Coordinate closely with PRDP personnel and the contractor regarding the completion of required documents for the release of funds. Also, continue the incessant follow-up with PRDP pending the release of funds.

We lauded Management for their diligent efforts in substantially minimizing unreconciled balances of Due to HDMF and Due to GSIS. Moreover, we reiterated prior years’ recommendations and the LCE agreed to:
Direct the MA to continue the thorough reconciliation between the amounts withheld or deducted and the amounts remitted, aiming to ascertain the accurate sums owed to GSIS/HDMF;
Instruct the MA to request confirmation of these balances from the relevant GOCC. Subsequently, if required, prepare any necessary adjusting entries; and
Instruct the HRMO to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of payroll preparation processes to ensure comprehensive inclusion of all employees in the remittance list as soon as their contributions and amortizations are deducted from their gross earnings.
We recommended that the LCE direct the MDRRMO to include all sources of LDRRMF in the preparation of LDRRMFIP in succeeding years as mandated by COA Circular NO. 2012-002.
observations.reason: No action taken by the Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE fill-up completely the mandatory positions to be assigned in the MDRRMO, if financially capable.  Otherwise, the instructions in Section 5.5 of the JMC shall be strictly observed.
observations.reason: The mandatory positions to be assigned in the MDRRMO were not yet completely filled-up due to Personal Services (PS) limitation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the MDRRM Officer to include unexpended balances from prior years and sources from other funds in the preparation of the LDRRMFIP.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this Report. | No Action taken by Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDRRM Council, through the MDRRM Officer develop a work plan that is more responsive to Disaster Risk Reduction Management-Climate Change Adaptation (DRRM-CCA) and implement the program, projects and activities (PPAs) identified until there is an evacuation center adequacy. And if the budget could consider, include the construction of evacuation center at least once a year.
observations.reason: Inadequate local fund for the acquisition of lot and for the construction of the evacuation centers

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management::

Implement CSC-DBM Joint Circular No. 2, Series of 2004 by issuing Executive/Administrative Order on the non-monetary remuneration for overtime services.
observations.reason: The employees need cash instead of CTO especially in this time of pandemic. Management will implement the recommendation when the situation returns to normal | Create a monitoring scheme on the part of HRMO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant (MA) and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer (OIC-MT) to prioritize the reconciliation of the CIB balances between the SLs, Cashbook and BS to come up with the correct balances of the said account in the FS; and
Direct the MA to conduct thorough review of records to determine the validity of stale checks,confirm cancellation thereofand make the necessary adjustments.

We recommended that Management require the MA and the Municipal Engineer (ME) to reconcile their records as to the status and costs incurred related to ongoing projects of the MGG.  Thereafter, require the MA to adjust the CIP accounts to reflect the correct balances thereof or make proper disclosure in the Notes to FS of the circumstances of the balances.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the LCE institute measures to further improve the financial performance of the market operations by increasing revenue collections through, but not limited to, the following:
Enhance the existing collection strategy of the OMT to ensure prompt payment of market dues by the stallholders;
Send out demand letters to all delinquent occupants to settle immediately their unpaid market dues. Otherwise, impose sanctions as provided under the Revenue Code and Municipal Ordinances; and
Offer the vacant market stalls to interested individuals/businesses through aggressive campaigns to maximize the income generation potential of the said stalls.

We recommended that the LCE:
Require computer literate employee/s from any MGG’s department/office to devise a basic computer program/system that could generate delinquent taxes.  If a system programmer is not available in the workplace, provide for reasonable funding to hire a competent job order worker to do the task. A personnel from the OMT shall be trained to operate the system closely supervised by the OIC-MT;
Require the OMT to conduct a comprehensive information drive/campaign on tax collection in all barangays. The information dissemination/campaign shall be extended to the whole municipality.  Also, require the OMT to post in conspicuous places tax information and send out notices to taxpayers as part of the tax campaign; and
Direct the MA to issue demand letters to all delinquent taxpayers to settle immediately their unpaid taxes. Otherwise, impose sanctions as provided under the pertinent Local Revenue Code and Municipal Ordinances.

We recommended that Management require the RCO to remit the full amount collected to the Liquidating Officer as required in the abovementioned provisions. We likewise recommended that the RCO maintain an updated cashbook for all collections and remittances made to provide proper record and to facilitate easy verification of his cash accountability at any given time.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management install necessary measures to strengthen the internal control in the collection system of the MGG as follows:
All RCOs shall be required to remit their collection in full at the close of each business day in accordance with the abovementioned rule;
The Liquidating Officer shall verify strictly the individual RCD of the RCOs, check the Statement of Accountable Forms and affix her signature on the back of the triplicate copy of the last Official Receipt issued to ensure that collections are remitted in full; and
Increase the bonding requirements of the RCOs pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the MGG.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to:
Verify the validity of the APs aged two years and more. Otherwise, cause the reversion thereof to the unappropriated surplus of the GF;
Inform in writing the concerned suppliers and contractors about their unclaimed retention money with the advice that the amount will be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the GF should there be no proper claim made within reasonable period; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 98 of PD No. 1445 on reversion of long outstanding payables.

We reiterated our previous year recommendations that the LCE direct the BAC, its Secretariat and Technical Working Group to:
Abide strictly with the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR and other relevant issuances on government procurement;
Exercise due diligence in the performance of their duties to ensure that all requirements of the procurement law are faithfully adhered; and
Undergo relevant trainings on government procurement that will enhance their capabilities in discharging their respective functions in an effective and efficient manner.

We recommended that the LCE require the MHRMO to:
Formulate uniform written policies and procedures in recruitment and engagement of JO personnel to strengthen the internal control pertaining thereto;
Conduct personnel review, assessment and evaluation of the existing manpower complement of the LGU to determine the necessity and propriety of hiring JO personnel; and
Use the appropriate forms of contract stipulating the duties and responsibilities of the JO, the expected output and deliverables and fair wage rates.
We also recommended that the MA and his staff continue to maintain the same efficiency and diligence towards making accurate, reliable and relevant financial reports.

We commended Management for the notable work accomplishments and we recommended that the MGG continue to implement projects with enthusiasm in compliance with Item No. 4 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 04, 2020.

We recommended that Management ensure that the identified priority PPAs are implemented as planned and the LDRRMF are utilized optimally within the targeted timeframes.  In such a manner, the in-house capability of the LGU to manage efficiently all types of emergencies and to achieve orderly transitions from response to a sustained recovery in case of calamity, will be strengthened.

We reiterated the prior year’s recommendation that the LCE instruct the MDRRM Officer to include unexpended balances from prior years, any continuing appropriations, and funds from other sources in the preparation of the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MDRRM Officer to:
Orient the local emergency response teams of management activities and procedures on disaster risk reduction and management;
Utilize the LDRRMF appropriation in providing proper trainings to the new members of the local emergency response teams.  Such trainings shall be on continuing basis to come up with suitably trained and competent personnel; and
Disseminate basic knowledge and education to representative of Barangays to strengthen the local disaster risk reduction and management system of the MGG.

We recommended that the LCE require the LSB to prepare strictly the SEF Budget based on the: (a) LSB three-year program;(b)recommendations of the DepEd PSDS and SDS; (c)priorities indicated in SIPs and DEDPs; and (d) other information from DepEd RO pursuant to DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, series of 2017to make the SEF Budget comprehensive and practical.  Also, furnish the AT of the SEF Budget including the aforesaid documents (a)–(d) for reference and review purposes.

We recommended that the LSB strictly comply with the requirements of Item No. 4.1.1.2 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1 series of 2017, which allows only the hiring of Utility workers and Security to be charged against SEF.

We recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the MA to perform reconciliation of the amounts withheld or deducted with the amounts remitted to determine the actual amounts due to GSIS and HDMF and request confirmation of such balance with the concerned GOCC. Thereafter, prepare the necessary adjusting entry, if any; and
Direct the Human Resource Department Head to improve the efficiency and data capture in payroll preparation so that all employees are captured in the remittance list once their contributions and amortizations are deducted from their gross earnings.

We recommended that Management require the RHU to:
Submit the PTRs and duly accomplished supporting documents to the Property Officer and OMA to facilitate the evaluation of the receipt and issuances/utilization of COVID-19 vaccines, ancillary supplies, and rice incentives and the recording of the remaining balances of unused inventories in the books and property records;
Coordinate with the PGS to secure the documents that contain the costs of the items donated as needed for the recording thereof; and
Ensure that the inventory procedures are aligned with the existing rules and regulations for proper compliance.

We recommended that Management require the MHO and RHU to plan properly the utilization and distribution of COVID-19 vaccines, especially those nearly expiring to prevent wastage thereof.  For this matter, we also recommend that the DOH and its procurement committees ensure that the vaccines are of good quality and hasten the distribution thereof to targeted LGUs to preserve their efficacy.

We recommended that Management continue their effective strategies in encouraging residents to get vaccinated, not just in their present campaign against COVID-19 but also in future efforts to combat other viruses. This is crucial for the protection and well-being of both individuals and the wider public.

We commended the Management, particularly the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO), the Property and Supply Office (PSO) and the Inventory Team for their commitment and efforts in conducting the annual physical inventory of all PPE items and reconciling the RPCPPE with the books and records of the MAO and PSO, thus ensuring a reliable balance of PPE accounts in the FS;

Nevertheless, we recommended that the LCE required the Municipal Accountant (MA) to reconcile the PPELC and the GL balances to arrive at reliable balances of the affected PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):

Require the Property Officer to maintain RLRN and Local Road Network Property Cards in standard form with complete information;

Require the MA to provide complete information in the Local Road Network Ledger Cards; and

Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Property Officer with the necessary information for the proper maintenance of the LRNPC.

We recommended that the LCE:

Require the Officer in Charge (OIC) – Municipal Treasurer (MT) and the Municipal Assessor to exert extra efforts in retrieving the land titles and other necessary documents as basis to establish ownership and determine proper valuation;

Allocate adequate budget for the titling of the subject land properties in the name of the Municipality to establish absolute ownership and reduce the risk of third party claims; and

Conduct proper evaluation of the 21 RPUs to facilitate the immediate recognition as assets of the Municipality considering the criteria provided under IPSAS No. 17.
observations.reason:
Retrieving the land titles and other necessary documents is on-going.

No budget is allocated for the titling of the subject land properties for CY 2022.

Evaluation of 21 RPUs is on-going.
Policy guidelines on hiring of JO personnel are not yet established.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), its Secretariat and Technical Working Group (TWG) to:

Abide strictly with the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR and other relevant issuances on government procurement;

Exercise due diligence in the performance of assigned duties to ensure that all requirements of the procurement law are faithfully adhered to particularly during post-qualification of bidders; and

Undergo relevant trainings on government procurement that will enhance capabilities in discharging respective functions in an effective and efficient manner.

We also recommended that the LCE required the OIC-MT to refrain from notarizing contracts entered into by the MGG to avoid conflict of interest.
observations.reason: New BAC members have not yet attended trainings related to government procurement.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE institute measures to further improve the financial performance of the market operations by increasing revenue collections through, but not limited to, the following:

Enhance the existing collection strategy of the MTO to ensure prompt payment of market dues by the stallholders;


Send out demand letters to all delinquent occupants to settle immediately their unpaid market dues. Otherwise, impose sanctions as provided under the Revenue Code and Municipal Ordinances; and

We likewise recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan review the existing Municipal Ordinances related to Gubat Public Market operations with the end view of giving consideration to stakeholders economically affected by COVID-19 pandemic but ensuring the stability, if not increase in the revenue collections.

observations.reason:
Uncollected market stall rentals increased to ₱13,300,333.12 as of December 31, 2022.

Delayed issuance of demand letters due to lack of MTO personnel.

Significant increase of vacant market stalls were noted since delinquent stallholders were not able to afford the new rates of the New Public Market.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management execute a valid MOA for all future fund transfers in compliance with the provisions of the LGC of 1991, COA Circular No. 94-013 and COA Circular No. 2012-001to ensure effective and efficient implementation of the projects.  The MOA shall contain the requisites previously discussed.

We recommended that the LCE:

Prepare the necessary adjustments to reconcile the MAO and barangay records. Thereafter, a periodic reconciliation of both records shall be conducted; and

Require the liquidation of financial assistance granted to the barangays in compliance with the provisions of the MOA by demanding submission of liquidation reports duly signed by the Barangay Treasurer and the Punong Barangay and supported with complete documentation per COA Circular No. 2012-001. Also, demand the return/refund of unutilized funds.
observations.reason:
Reconciliation of Due from LGUs accounts is on-going.

Some of the liquidation reports were not yet submitted by the Barangays.

observations.recommendation: We commended Management for the notable work accomplishments and we recommended that it will continue to implement projects with enthusiasm in compliance with Item No. 4 of the DBM DOF DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 04, 2020.

We commended Management for continuously extending assistance to the National Government through faithful compliance of its duties as withholding and remitting agent of taxes in accordance with tax laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the LCE require the MA to conduct detailed review and analysis of the un-reconciled balances in the inter-agency payable accounts, including confirmation with the respective government agencies to determine the correct balance of the said accounts and thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books.

We likewise recommended the immediate remittance of amounts withheld to the concerned government agencies and ensure that monthly payments correspond to exact amounts withheld to avoid incurrence of penalties and surcharges so that Municipal officials and employees could enjoy the benefits due them.

Furthermore, we recommended that the LCE enjoin the newly-hired employees to submit immediately their GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth ID numbers to ensure the timely remittance of their withheld contributions.

observations.reason:
Revision of 10-year Municipal SWM Plan is on-going.

The MGG needs to purchase equipment for waste diversion.

Initial site assessment of DENR-EMB was conducted last February 2022.

37 out of 42 Barangays have operational Barangay MRF.

We recommended that the LCE and the Municipal Solid Waste Management (SWM) Board:

Provide and implement the solutions to address the identified 13 issues embodied in the submitted SWM plan 2018-2027; and

Implement the garbage collection fee as provided in Section 1, Article N of the 2005 Revenue Code of the Municipality.

Prioritize provision/ allocation of enough budget for full implementation of SWM PPAs to address all the identified key issues as provided in the Municipal SWM plan.

Schedule barangay consultation to discuss garbage collection fee per household

Enhance its current system of preparing the succeeding year’s revenue projections to come up with realistic targets that are backed up by current trends, verifiable figures and data; and

The Municipal Treasurer perform periodic review and collection monitoring to each revenue account especially to identify revenue sources which were not able to meet the targets; intensify collection strategies; institute appropriate action and make adjustments, when necessary.

There is a need to update the data to come up with a verifiable figure and reliable data

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer exert more effort to gather the necessary documents to complete the requirements for the processing of the Transfer Certificates of Title of the subject real property in favor of the Municipality. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:

Require the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over Cash.  The unrecorded disbursements/deposits/book error be booked up in the General Journal by drawing a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) duly supported with complete and proper documents;

Require the Municipal Accountant to coordinate closely with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to ensure that all disbursements/deposits for a given period are booked up and recorded in the books of the Municipality. In addition, bank confirmation be made regularly to validate actual existence of the accounts; and

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Request the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) of the Municipality to authorize the Local Chief Executive to transfer all completed projects implemented which were already under the custody and control of the different end-users/beneficiaries and prepare the required Invoice and Receipt – General Form 30-A to support the transfer; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to derecognize from the agency books of accounts the cost or value of the properties if already transferred to the respective end-users/beneficiaries in order that the subject PPE accounts would be correctly and reliably presented in the financial statements.

Review the payable accounts and reconcile the same with other related accounts, if possible. If the review shows that these payables that had been outstanding for more than two years, and no actual claims by creditors, have been made, or if these were covered by perfected contracts on record, revert the same to the Unappropriated Surplus of the general fund pursuant to Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445.
Likewise, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivable accounts as required under Item No. 7 of COA Circular No. 2016-005. For those unsettled receivables supported with legal and valid documents, send demand letters to concerned agencies/association/personnel for them to settle immediately, while those accounts where settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained, we recommended that Management follow the guidelines and procedures on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that Management:

a. request the concerned officials and employees to liquidate/settle immediately
their outstanding cash advances by demanding the submission of duly
accomplished liquidation reports. Otherwise, withhold any money due them;

b. strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to ensure
timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of balances of
cash advances;

c. immediately stop the grant of additional cash advances to all officers and
employees with unliquidated cash advances until such time that previous cash
advances are fully settled; and

d. institute appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those erring accountable
officers/emplayees for continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules
and regulations on the granting, utilization and liguidation of cash advances,
if warranted.

We recommended that Management remit immediately the remaining balance of
#£108,275.00 to the Bureau of Treasury to comply with Supreme Court’s Decision on
DAP.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that Management consider the inclusion of infrasiructure
projects in the preparation of the APP of the Municipality and refrain from commencing
any procurement unless it is in accordance with the approved APP in compliance with
RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendation.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that Management:

a. require the BAC, the Secretariat and the TWG fo strictly abide by the
standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its Revised IRR and other relevant laws
on government procurement;

b. require them also to exercise due diligence in the performance of their duties
1o ensure that all requirements of the procurement law are faithfully adhered
fo;

c. direct the Municipal Accountant to observe the requirements of COA Circular
No. 2012-001 at all times o avoid suspension or disallowance in audit; and

d. require all members of the BAC, the Secretariat and the TWG to undergo
relevant trainings on government procurement that will enhance their
capabilities in discharging their respective fimctions in an effective and
efficient manner.

Also, we recommended that Management observe the requirements of COA
Circular No. 2009-001 regarding the submission of copies of approved contracts and
their supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) working days from perfection
thereof.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that Management ensure that the basic requirements for the
extension of contract time are complied with in accordance with Annex E of the Revised
IRR of RA No. 9184 and that proper documentation is observed at all times to warrant
the propriety of the transactions of the Municipality.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that Management exercise judicious spending of resources to
ensure maximum utilization of the same as emphasized in Section 2 of PD No. 1445.
Proper planning should be considered by Management to avoid wastage of limited
resources and to ensure effective and efficient implementation of programs and projects.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the LSB comply stricily with
the requirements of Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular
Nos. 01 5. 1998, 01-4 s. 2000 and 01-B 5. 2001, as follows:

a. identify the PPAs that reflect an equitable distribution of the mandated
priority expenses and refrain from using the SEF outside the scope for which
it was established; and

b. maximize the implementation of the identified and prioritized PPAs to ensure
efficient utilization of the fund so that its ebjectives are fully achieved.

In addition, we recommended that Management consider the following measures:

a. close moniloring of the budget to anticipate the timely re-alignment of funds
for other allowable PPAs in lieu of those that cannot be implemented; and

b. preparation of monthly statement of cash flow analysis to monitor the
available fund so that no priority PPAs (low-priced) be deterred from being
implemented,

Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that Management encourage the LDRRMO to exert more effort
in implementing actions and measures pertaining to all aspects of disaster risk reduction
and management towards building a disaster-resilient LGU and communities.

Also, we recommended that Management closely monitor the implementation. of
all PPAs under the LDRRMF to facilitate corrective action, if any, and the timely
reprogramming and reallocation of the fund for its optimum utilization.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We reiterated our prior year's audit recommendation that Management:

a. follow up the approval of the 10-year Municipal SWM Plan 1o serve as guide
Jor the efficient management of solid waste generated within the
Maunicipality’s jurisdiction;

b. prioritize solid waste management programs and projects by
providing/allocating enough budget for their full implementation;

c. strictly comply with R4 No. 9003 on the establishment of solid waste facility;
and

d. exert continuous effort in educating the people of Gubat the importance of
solid waste management.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendations. There is already a
move to address the concemns regarding solid waste management. In fact, the
Municipality is expecting a donation of back hoe and dump truck from the United States
of America. Also, Management is planning to coordinate with the barangay officials
regarding establishment of MRF in all barangays.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management require the
Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort in preparing and
submitting on time the subject accounts and financial reports including the supporting
documents. We suggested that such activities be properly monitored so that reports are
submitted promptly in accordance with the salient provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA
Circular No. 2009-006.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that Management strictly comply
with DBM ard DILG JMC No. 2011-1, as follows:

a. maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund and implement
the programs, prajects and activities within the targeted timeframe to achieve
the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment
of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all;

b. develop a concrete plan that will articulate the program, project and activity,
timeline, expected deliverables for each phase, key milestones, etc.; and

c. adopt sirategies to conduct periodic monitoring and evaluation of reported
accomplishments to guarantee to the stakeholders the accuracy and reliability
of information that are relevant in decision-making.

Also, we recommended that Management ensure that the 20 per cent Development
Fund is solely used for development projects and no amount is transferred to other funds.

Management agreed to comply with the recommendations.

Fund Utilization Report and Subsidiary Ledger of  each project/program  be reconciled monthly to validate the correctness of the balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned exert more efforts to collect the rentals and business  taxes  from delinquent market stall occupants in order to generate additional income that will be used for the implementation of other projects/programs of the municipality.
